speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] In Marghita. On the right side, with a pocket on her hand is my mother. Her name, Irina Farkas. Her sister, on the left side with the flower on her hand, Gisella Farkas.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number five, April 21, 1995, interview with--

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in 1927, Simleul-Silvaniei. On the right side is my father, Eugene Berkovits. On the left side is my mother. Her maiden name, Irina Farkas. On that picture, I am. The picture was taken in 1936, Simleul-Silvaniei, Romania. And probably I wore a light blue dress, with a colorful ball.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1936, I was six, seven years old. But this picture looks younger. So I don't know. The picture probably dated wrong. On, on the picture, front of the-- on the picture what is taken now, it's-- was taken in 1939, Marghita. From the right side is my uncle Rosenfeld Armin, who married my mother's older sister, Mathilda Farkas. In the middle is my first cousin--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is the woman? Your uncle's on the far right, with the Russian hat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the woman next to him is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mathilda Farkas, her-- his wife, Rosenfeld Mathilda. On the middle is their son, Eliezer Rosenfeld. And after Eliezer Rosenfeld, in the-- it's a little shade on her face-- is my mother, Irina Farkas Berkovits. And on the left side, the last one is my father, Eugene Berkovits.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939, In February-- I'm sorry. In February 1939, the picture was taken. This picture-- this picture was taken in 1942. This is the only brother I had. His name, Adalbert Berkovits. And when they emptied our home, somebody found on the street and mutilated as it is, they returned to me in 1945, about in September, after I returned from the war. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] He was so handsome. [SIGHS] I'm wondering how he'll look now. On this picture, this picture was taken in 1945, July. And was taken in Hillersleben, where my second cousin-- my mother's first cousin, Horstein Bondi-- took us. This was a German officer's recreation place. Here, I was treated by the American doctors, because I was coughing constantly. On the right side is Yboia Farkas. Next to her, myself, Elly Berkovits Gross. In the middle is Horstein Bondi, my mother's first cousin. Next to him, towards the left, is Eva Farkas. And the last one is [NON-ENGLISH] from Budapest. I'm sorry, I don't remember her second name. This official paper was given to-- to me in Hillersleben, by the organization who kept us over there in Hillersleben. And this my official paper, that I was there in June, 1945. This official certificate was given to me in Praga, in 19-- July, 1945, when we were traveling to go home from Germany. This is the inside of the document what was given to me in Praga on July 30, 1945, at the Roma-- Czechoslovakian consulate. Surprise how I-- This [NON-ENGLISH] what I got when we crossed the border, going-- coming home from the Holocaust, in August 11, 1945. I received at the border. That was a certificate that I was able to travel in Romania, without payment, without pay. It was a free ticket. This picture was taken on June 27, 1946, when I got married to Ernest Gross. And he's my husband for 49 years. We hope we live a few more years, until our grandchildren get married. That-- my son was in the Israeli Army during the Gulf War in 1991. He was an active soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have to--please give me his full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: His-- his name is-- I'm sorry. Tiberiu Gross, our son. In 1991, he was serving in the Israeli Army as an active soldier. On the right side-- the picture was taken in 1990, in the summer. We were in a beautiful park. The right side is our daughter, Agneta Gross Weiss, her married name. And on the left side is our son, Tiberiu Gross. On this picture is my whole family, dearest to my-- my heart. On the right side--

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in 1990, when our-- when our older grandson was bar mitzvah. On the right side on the top is the grandson's father, Tiberiu Weiss. He's a lawyer. Next to it, our son, Tiberiu Gross. After, my husband, Ernest Gross. I sitting on the left side, myself, Elly Gross. Next to me, the bar mitzvah boy, Gabriel Weiss. After him is our daughter, Agneta Gross Weiss. And the little one is our youngest grandson, Jonathan Weiss.
